Thank you for the revised offer. I have to say the price might be a little high for us. I was wondering whether there is any wiggle room.
We could look at a small adjustment, but the penalty clause would need to stay as it is.
I understand. To be honest, the penalty clause is our one sticking point. We have a couple of reservations about the notice period.
That is a fair point. What did you have in mind?
It might be worth shortening the notice period a little. We would be amenable to meeting you on price if you could be flexible there. That feels constructive for both sides.
I can live with that, with one caveat: we confirm it in writing by Friday. Then we have a tentative deal.
We have one or two reservations about this clause.
It might be worth revisiting the notice period.
I can live with that, with one caveat: confirm it in writing.
Protect the goodwill, and the deal protects itself.

Good morning, everyone. Before we sit down with the supplier this afternoon, let me set the tone for how we negotiate. Last quarter we decided on the dual-supplier plan, so today is about the terms, not the choice. I would ask you to keep the language diplomatic. When we push back on price, we do not say the offer is unacceptable. We say it might be a little high, and we ask whether there is any wiggle room. If a clause is a sticking point, we do not reject it outright. We say we have one or two reservations, and we suggest a constructive alternative. Remember, the goal is a contract both sides can sign, so we protect the goodwill. Understate the problems, soften the demands, and add a caveat where you need one. If the room gets tense, defuse it with a question rather than a statement. A supplier who feels respected is a supplier who is amenable. Let us hammer out the final terms without anyone leaving the table unhappy.

Hi Marcus, this is Fabiana with a quick update after the call with the supplier. Overall it went well, and I think we are close. There is just one sticking point left. They were a little reluctant on the penalty clause, so I softened our position and said we might be open to a shorter notice period if they would meet us on price. They seem amenable to that. I added one caveat: any change has to be confirmed in writing before Friday. To be honest, the tone was very constructive throughout, and there is still a bit of wiggle room on the delivery schedule. I would suggest we not push too hard on the last point, just to protect the goodwill. If you could review my notes, I will hammer out the wording tomorrow. Thanks, and talk soon.

Marcus sets the tone and asks the team to keep the language diplomatic. Fabiana opens by understating the problem and asking whether there is any wiggle room on price. The supplier raises a sticking point on the penalty clause. Fabiana softens the demand and offers a constructive alternative with one caveat. Both sides protect the goodwill and hammer out the final terms.
